question: A student is asked to list the primary anatomical sites where normal microbial communities colonize on the human body. Which of the following lists best matches the typical colonization sites described in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Define microbiota/normal microbiota and identify sites of their colonization on/in the human body.

options.text: Skin; nose; mouth; large intestine; vagina
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture lists skin (especially moist areas), respiratory tract (nose and oropharynx), digestive tract (mouth and large intestine), and genital tract (vagina) as typical colonization sites.

options.text: Blood; cerebrospinal fluid; synovial fluid; deep tissues
options.rationale: Wrong: These sites are usually sterile per the lecture; if they were colonized it would indicate infection rather than normal microbiota.

options.text: Heart; liver; spleen; pancreas; kidneys
options.rationale: Wrong: Internal organs are normally sterile according to the lecture; colonization here would represent invasive infection.

options.text: Middle ear; cornea; myocardium; bone marrow
options.rationale: Wrong: These are not listed as normal colonization sites in the lecture; their involvement typically indicates pathology.

options.text: Bronchioles; alveoli; pericardium; vitreous humor
options.rationale: Wrong: Lower respiratory tract and sterile body fluids are normally sterile per the lecture; colonization would be abnormal.

question: Which structural feature most reliably distinguishes prokaryotic cells from eukaryotic cells as emphasized in the lecture slides?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the classification of medically important microorganisms based on their structures.

options.text: Absence of a membrane-bound nucleus in prokaryotes
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture contrasts prokaryotes (no nucleus) with eukaryotes (nucleus with membrane) as a primary distinguishing structural feature.

options.text: Presence of ergosterol in prokaryotic plasma membranes
options.rationale: Wrong: Ergosterol is characteristic of fungal eukaryotes per the lecture; prokaryotes generally lack sterols except Mycoplasma.

options.text: Multiple linear chromosomes in prokaryotes
options.rationale: Wrong: Prokaryotes usually have a single circular chromosome; multiple linear chromosomes are a eukaryotic trait.

options.text: 80S ribosomes present in prokaryotes
options.rationale: Wrong: Prokaryotes have 70S ribosomes; 80S ribosomes are typical of eukaryotes as noted in the lecture.

options.text: Membrane-bound organelles common in prokaryotes
options.rationale: Wrong: Prokaryotes lack membrane-bound organelles; presence of such organelles indicates eukaryotes per the lecture.

question: Which of the following definitions matches the lecture's definition of 'microbiome'?
cited_learning_objective: Define microbiota/normal microbiota and identify sites of their colonization on/in the human body.

options.text: Aggregate collection of microbial genomes in a microbiota
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture defines microbiome as the aggregate collection of microbial genomes/microorganisms in the microbiota.

options.text: Individual microbial species that always cause disease
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture defines microbiota as communities that can be commensal; not all species cause disease.

options.text: Only the culturable bacteria from stool samples
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture's microbiome concept includes all microbial genomes, not just culturable bacteria from stool.

options.text: Host genetic sequences that influence microbial growth
options.rationale: Wrong: Microbiome refers to microbial genomes, not host genetic sequences; host genetics may influence microbiota but are distinct.

options.text: A single dominant species present at a body site
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture describes microbiome as an aggregate collection, not a single dominant species; core and secondary microbiome concepts cover shared and unique species.

question: Which of the following best describes a core microbiome as defined in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Define microbiota/normal microbiota and identify sites of their colonization on/in the human body.

options.text: Species commonly shared among individuals at a body site
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture defines core microbiome as commonly shared microbial species among individuals at specific body sites.

options.text: Species unique to a single individual at all body sites
options.rationale: Wrong: That describes individual-specific or secondary microbiome elements, not the core microbiome.

options.text: All pathogenic organisms found in a population
options.rationale: Wrong: Core microbiome refers to shared commensal species, not exclusively pathogens.

options.text: Transient microbes acquired during illness only
options.rationale: Wrong: Transient microbes are not the core microbiome; core species are stable and commonly shared.

options.text: Only viral genomes present in stool samples
options.rationale: Wrong: Core microbiome includes bacteria and other microbes shared at a site, not only viral genomes.

question: According to the lecture, which of the following is a recognized beneficial role of normal flora?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Synthesis of vitamin K and biotin by gut microbes
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture lists vitamin K and biotin synthesis as nutritional benefits provided by normal flora.

options.text: Production of ergosterol to strengthen host membranes
options.rationale: Wrong: Ergosterol is a fungal membrane sterol; the lecture does not describe microbes producing ergosterol to strengthen host membranes.

options.text: Permanent colonization of blood to prevent infection
options.rationale: Wrong: Blood is normally sterile per the lecture; colonization would indicate pathology, not benefit.

options.text: Conversion of host DNA to microbial genomes
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture does not describe microbes converting host DNA; this is not a beneficial role.

options.text: Secretion of host antibodies to neutralize pathogens
options.rationale: Wrong: Host antibodies are produced by the immune system; microbes stimulate IgA but do not secrete host antibodies themselves.

question: Which lecture term refers specifically to live organisms taken to confer a health benefit when ingested?
cited_learning_objective: Define microbiota/normal microbiota and identify sites of their colonization on/in the human body.

options.text: Probiotic
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture defines probiotic as a live organism that when ingested is believed to provide benefit to the host.

options.text: Prebiotic
options.rationale: Wrong: Prebiotic refers to diet or supplements that encourage growth of helpful microbes, not live organisms themselves.

options.text: Metabolome
options.rationale: Wrong: Metabolomics studies metabolic products; it does not denote live organisms given to the host.

options.text: Core microbiota
options.rationale: Wrong: Core microbiota are commonly shared species at a site, not live supplements administered to a host.

options.text: Commensal vaccine
options.rationale: Wrong: This is not a lecture term; the lecture uses probiotic for live beneficial organisms.

question: A patient develops severe diarrhea after broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy. Based on the lecture's examples of dysbiosis, which organism is most likely responsible for antibiotic-associated colitis in this scenario?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Clostridium difficile overgrowth after antibiotics
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture cites antibiotic-associated colitis from C. difficile proliferation as an example of dysbiosis.

options.text: Streptococcus mutans causing tooth demineralization
options.rationale: Wrong: S. mutans is associated with dental caries in the mouth; it is not the classic cause of post-antibiotic colitis.

options.text: Lactobacillus overgrowth causing systemic sepsis
options.rationale: Wrong: Lactobacilli are protective in the vagina and gut; overgrowth causing systemic sepsis is not the lecture example for antibiotic-associated colitis.

options.text: Bacteroides fragilis causing infective endocarditis
options.rationale: Wrong: B. fragilis is linked to intra-abdominal abscesses; endocarditis is not the typical post-antibiotic colitis cause.

options.text: Propionibacterium acnes causing gastrointestinal ulcers
options.rationale: Wrong: P. acnes (Cutibacterium) is a skin commensal linked to acne; it is not implicated in antibiotic-associated colitis per the lecture.

question: Which lecture concept explains why colonization by commensal microbes can prevent invasion by pathogens?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Competition for space and nutrients by normal flora
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture states that competition for space and nutrients by normal flora helps keep invaders out.

options.text: Production of host IgG by commensal bacteria
options.rationale: Wrong: Commensals stimulate host IgA and immune development, but they do not produce host IgG themselves.

options.text: Direct phagocytosis of pathogens by commensal microbes
options.rationale: Wrong: Commensals do not phagocytose pathogens; they inhibit invaders via competition and antimicrobial secretion.

options.text: Conversion of pathogens into nonviable spores
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture does not describe commensals converting pathogens into spores; this is not a listed mechanism.

options.text: Secretion of host mucin by commensal bacteria
options.rationale: Wrong: Mucin is produced by host epithelial cells; commensals help maintain mucosal barriers but do not secrete host mucin.

question: Which of the following lecture-defined terms refers to the study of protein products of the microbiome population?
cited_learning_objective: Define microbiota/normal microbiota and identify sites of their colonization on/in the human body.

options.text: Proteomics
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture defines proteomics as the study of protein products of the microbiome population.

options.text: Metabolomics
options.rationale: Wrong: Metabolomics studies metabolic products, not proteins; proteomics specifically addresses proteins.

options.text: Genomics
options.rationale: Wrong: Genomics studies genomes; the lecture uses microbiome to refer to aggregate genomes, while proteomics is protein-focused.

options.text: Transcriptomics
options.rationale: Wrong: Transcriptomics studies RNA transcripts; the lecture specifically names proteomics for proteins.

options.text: Phenomics
options.rationale: Wrong: Phenomics refers to phenotypes broadly; the lecture specifically lists proteomics for protein products.

question: A patient with poor dental hygiene develops transient bacteremia after a tooth extraction and later presents with fever and a new heart murmur. Based on the lecture's examples, which oral organism is most likely to colonize damaged heart valves and cause endocarditis?
cited_learning_objective: Provide examples of disease states related to microbiota at different anatomic sites.

options.text: Streptococcus mutans entering bloodstream and adhering
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture explains S. mutans can enter the bloodstream after dental procedures and colonize damaged or prosthetic heart valves causing infective endocarditis.

options.text: Neisseria species causing subacute valvular infection
options.rationale: Wrong: Neisseria are oral commensals but the lecture specifically highlights S. mutans for dental-procedure-associated endocarditis; Neisseria would be less likely unless specific evidence suggested it.

options.text: Propionibacterium acnes causing acute valvular destruction
options.rationale: Wrong: Propionibacterium (Cutibacterium) is a skin commensal linked to acne; it is not the classic oral organism causing endocarditis after dental work per the lecture.

options.text: Haemophilus species causing immediate valve colonization
options.rationale: Wrong: Haemophilus can be part of oral flora but the lecture emphasizes S. mutans in dental-procedure bacteremia leading to endocarditis; Haemophilus would require different epidemiologic clues.

options.text: Bacteroides species causing subacute valvular infection
options.rationale: Wrong: Bacteroides are anaerobes of the gut; they are not the typical oral organism implicated in post-dental endocarditis per the lecture.

question: Which lecture slide concept best explains why germ-free mice show immune deficiencies and digestive problems?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Normal flora stimulate immune development and tolerance
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture notes germ-free mice have immune deficiencies, illustrating that normal flora stimulate immune development and tolerance.

options.text: Absence of flora increases vitamin K synthesis
options.rationale: Wrong: Absence of flora would decrease vitamin K synthesis; the lecture links lack of flora to deficiencies, not increases.

options.text: Germ-free state causes overproduction of mucin
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture does not state mucin overproduction in germ-free mice; it emphasizes immune and digestive dysfunction from lack of microbiota.

options.text: Sterile gut increases colonization resistance
options.rationale: Wrong: Sterile gut lacks colonization resistance; the lecture indicates normal flora provide protection against invaders.

options.text: Germ-free mice have enhanced antibody diversity
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture reports antibody deficiencies in germ-free mice, not enhanced diversity.

question: Which of the following lecture-described mechanisms is NOT a way normal flora help prevent pathogen invasion?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Direct secretion of host IgA by commensal bacteria
options.rationale: Correct: This is NOT a mechanism; the lecture states commensals stimulate host IgA production but do not secrete host IgA themselves.

options.text: Physical occupancy of mucosal surfaces by commensals
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture lists physical occupancy as a mechanism by which normal flora keep invaders out.

options.text: Secretion of bacteriocins or antimicrobials by flora
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture describes secretion of antibiotics or bacteriocins by commensals as a protective mechanism.

options.text: Maintenance of local environmental factors like pH
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture notes maintaining environmental factors (e.g., pH) as a way flora prevent invasion.

options.text: Competition for nutrients limiting pathogen growth
options.rationale: Wrong: Competition for space and nutrients is explicitly listed in the lecture as protective.

question: Which slide-defined group of organisms includes unicellular eukaryotes typically 15–60 µm in size?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the classification of medically important microorganisms based on their structures.

options.text: Protozoa
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture lists protozoa as unicellular eukaryotes typically 15–60 µm in size.

options.text: Helminths
options.rationale: Wrong: Helminths are multicellular eukaryotes and can be much larger (up to meters), not unicellular 15–60 µm organisms.

options.text: Viruses
options.rationale: Wrong: Viruses are subcellular and much smaller (nm scale), not 15–60 µm unicellular eukaryotes.

options.text: Bacteria
options.rationale: Wrong: Bacteria are prokaryotes typically 1–20 µm, not eukaryotic protozoa sized 15–60 µm.

options.text: Fungi (mold form)
options.rationale: Wrong: Filamentous fungi are multicellular molds or larger structures; the lecture distinguishes fungi from unicellular protozoa.

question: A microbiology slide shows an organism with peptidoglycan cell wall and 70S ribosomes. According to the lecture's structural comparisons, this organism most likely belongs to which group?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the classification of medically important microorganisms based on their structures.

options.text: Bacteria with peptidoglycan cell wall and 70S ribosomes
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture states bacteria have peptidoglycan cell walls and 70S ribosomes, distinguishing them from eukaryotes and viruses.

options.text: Viruses with obligate intracellular replication
options.rationale: Wrong: Viruses lack peptidoglycan and ribosomes; they are obligate intracellular and have nucleic acid genomes.

options.text: Fungi containing ergosterol and membrane organelles
options.rationale: Wrong: Fungi are eukaryotic with 80S ribosomes and ergosterol; they do not have 70S ribosomes or peptidoglycan.

options.text: Protozoa with multiple linear chromosomes
options.rationale: Wrong: Protozoa are eukaryotic and have membrane-bound nuclei and 80S ribosomes, not 70S ribosomes.

options.text: Helminths with rigid peptidoglycan exoskeleton
options.rationale: Wrong: Helminths are multicellular eukaryotes and do not have peptidoglycan; this option contradicts lecture structural descriptions.

question: Which lecture example illustrates an opportunistic infection arising from normal skin flora introduced into subcutaneous tissue?
cited_learning_objective: Provide examples of disease states related to microbiota at different anatomic sites.

options.text: Skin popping leading to soft tissue infection by skin flora
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture describes 'skin popping' (injecting drugs under the skin) breaking the barrier and causing soft tissue infections from normal skin flora.

options.text: Dental plaque causing tooth demineralization
options.rationale: Wrong: Dental plaque and tooth demineralization are oral microbiota effects, not skin flora introduced into subcutaneous tissue.

options.text: Vaginal Lactobacillus overgrowth causing vaginitis
options.rationale: Wrong: Vaginitis from Lactobacillus imbalance is a GU tract dysbiosis example, not skin flora subcutaneous infection.

options.text: Gut perforation causing urinary tract infection
options.rationale: Wrong: Gut perforation can cause intra-abdominal infection; it is not the skin flora subcutaneous infection example from the lecture.

options.text: Nasal commensals causing chronic sinusitis without barrier breach
options.rationale: Wrong: Chronic sinusitis involves respiratory flora but the lecture's opportunistic skin example specifically involves barrier breach like skin popping.

question: Which lecture slide concept best accounts for increased susceptibility to obesity and metabolic changes linked to microbiota alterations?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Dysbiosis altering metabolic signaling and inflammation
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture links dysbiosis to susceptibility to obesity and metabolic changes via altered microbial metabolites and inflammatory signals.

options.text: Direct microbial infection of adipose tissue causing weight gain
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture discusses microbiome-mediated metabolic effects, not direct infection of adipose tissue as the mechanism.

options.text: Loss of skin flora leading to increased caloric intake
options.rationale: Wrong: Skin flora loss is not described as causing obesity; the lecture emphasizes gut microbiota effects on metabolism.

options.text: Viral colonization of muscle increasing fat deposition
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture does not describe viral muscle colonization as a mechanism for obesity; it focuses on gut microbiome interactions.

options.text: Increased oral flora causing systemic insulin resistance
options.rationale: Wrong: While oral flora can influence systemic health, the lecture specifically highlights gut microbiota dysbiosis in obesity susceptibility.

question: Which of the following lecture-described outcomes can result from intestinal perforation?
cited_learning_objective: Provide examples of disease states related to microbiota at different anatomic sites.

options.text: Dumping of intestinal microbes into sterile tissues causing infection
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture notes that perforation of the intestine will dump many organisms into normally sterile sites, causing infection.

options.text: Immediate sterilization of the peritoneal cavity
options.rationale: Wrong: Perforation introduces microbes into sterile spaces, causing contamination and infection rather than sterilization.

options.text: Enhanced mucosal immunity preventing abscess formation
options.rationale: Wrong: Perforation typically overwhelms defenses and leads to abscesses or peritonitis, not enhanced protection.

options.text: Selective elimination of anaerobes only
options.rationale: Wrong: Perforation releases a mixed flora including anaerobes and aerobes; it does not selectively eliminate anaerobes.

options.text: Conversion of gut flora into commensals in peritoneum
options.rationale: Wrong: Gut flora in the peritoneum act as pathogens causing infection; they do not become harmless commensals in sterile tissues.

question: Which lecture-listed genus is a common Gram-negative rod found in the gut microbiota and implicated in intra-abdominal infections after perforation?
cited_learning_objective: Provide examples of disease states related to microbiota at different anatomic sites.

options.text: Bacteroides species associated with intra-abdominal abscesses
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture names Bacteroides species as anaerobes that cause intra-abdominal abscesses after intestinal perforation.

options.text: Streptococcus sanguinis causing intra-abdominal abscesses
options.rationale: Wrong: S. sanguinis is an oral Gram-positive coccus; the lecture links Bacteroides to intra-abdominal abscesses.

options.text: Corynebacterium species causing peritonitis after perforation
options.rationale: Wrong: Corynebacterium are skin commensals; the lecture emphasizes Bacteroides and other gut anaerobes in intra-abdominal infections.

options.text: Propionibacterium acnes causing gut-derived abscesses
options.rationale: Wrong: Propionibacterium (Cutibacterium) is a skin organism; it is not the primary gut anaerobe implicated in intra-abdominal abscesses per the lecture.

options.text: Neisseria species causing peritonitis after bowel rupture
options.rationale: Wrong: Neisseria are respiratory/oral commensals; the lecture identifies Bacteroides and intestinal bacteria as causes of peritonitis.

question: Which lecture point explains why antibiotic therapy can predispose to pathogen overgrowth in the gut?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Antibiotics deplete protective microbiota allowing pathogen expansion
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture shows antibiotics can deplete normal microbiota, reducing colonization resistance and permitting pathogen overgrowth (e.g., C. difficile).

options.text: Antibiotics increase mucin production preventing pathogen growth
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture does not state antibiotics increase mucin to prevent growth; rather antibiotics disrupt protective flora.

options.text: Antibiotics convert commensals into obligate intracellular pathogens
options.rationale: Wrong: Antibiotics do not convert commensals into intracellular pathogens; they alter community structure enabling opportunists to expand.

options.text: Antibiotics selectively kill viruses allowing bacterial overgrowth
options.rationale: Wrong: Antibiotics target bacteria, not viruses; the lecture emphasizes loss of bacterial diversity leading to opportunistic bacterial overgrowth.

options.text: Antibiotics permanently sterilize the gut preventing recolonization
options.rationale: Wrong: Antibiotics transiently deplete microbiota; the lecture does not claim permanent sterilization, and recolonization or dysbiosis can follow.

question: A researcher measures stool microbial metabolites and protein products to study host–microbe interactions. According to lecture terminology, which two fields correspond to these analyses?
cited_learning_objective: Define microbiota/normal microbiota and identify sites of their colonization on/in the human body.

options.text: Proteomics for proteins; metabolomics for metabolites
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture defines proteomics as study of protein products and metabolomics as study of metabolomic products of the microbiome.

options.text: Genomics for proteins; transcriptomics for metabolites
options.rationale: Wrong: Genomics studies DNA and transcriptomics studies RNA; the lecture assigns proteins to proteomics and metabolites to metabolomics.

options.text: Phenomics for proteins; lipidomics for metabolites
options.rationale: Wrong: Phenomics is broad phenotype study and lipidomics targets lipids; the lecture specifically names proteomics and metabolomics for proteins and metabolites.

options.text: Metagenomics for proteins; proteomics for metabolites
options.rationale: Wrong: Metagenomics studies genomes of microbial communities; proteomics studies proteins, not metabolites.

options.text: Transcriptomics for proteins; metatranscriptomics for metabolites
options.rationale: Wrong: Transcriptomics concerns RNA transcripts; it does not directly measure proteins or metabolites as defined in the lecture.

question: Which lecture-described clinical example demonstrates that normal vaginal flora protect against overgrowth of pathogenic species?
cited_learning_objective: Provide examples of disease states related to microbiota at different anatomic sites.

options.text: Loss of Lactobacilli leading to bacterial vaginosis
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture states that decreased Lactobacillus numbers in the vagina allow overgrowth of pathogens like Gardnerella vaginalis, causing BV.

options.text: Overgrowth of Enterococcus causing urinary incontinence
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture does not link Enterococcus overgrowth to urinary incontinence; it specifically highlights Lactobacillus loss and BV.

options.text: Staphylococcus epidermidis causing vulvar ulcers
options.rationale: Wrong: S. epidermidis is a skin commensal; the lecture's GU tract dysbiosis example centers on Lactobacillus and BV.

options.text: Propionibacterium acnes causing cervicitis
options.rationale: Wrong: P. acnes is a skin organism associated with acne; cervicitis from vaginal dysbiosis is not attributed to it in the lecture.

options.text: Klebsiella species preventing yeast overgrowth
options.rationale: Wrong: Klebsiella are potential pathogens; the lecture does not describe them as protective against yeast in the vagina.

question: Which lecture slide best supports the statement: 'You can change your microbiota but not your genetic code'?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Microbiota are modifiable and influence health outcomes
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture explicitly states you can't change your genetic code but you can change your microbiota, which affects health and disease.

options.text: Genetic mutations are the primary drivers of microbiome composition
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture emphasizes modifiable microbiota factors (diet, antibiotics), not immutable genetic mutations as primary drivers.

options.text: Microbiota composition is fixed at birth and unchangeable
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture notes microbiota are in flux and influenced by age, diet, hygiene, etc., so they are changeable.

options.text: Host genome determines all microbial species present
options.rationale: Wrong: While genetics can influence microbiota, the lecture stresses environmental and lifestyle factors that can change microbiota.

options.text: Microbiome cannot be altered by dietary interventions
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture mentions prebiotics and diet affecting microbiota, so dietary interventions can alter the microbiome.

question: A clinician considers fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) for a patient with recurrent C. difficile infection. Based on the lecture's case example, what principle underlies the therapeutic rationale for FMT?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Restoring healthy microbial community to outcompete pathogen
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture's fecal transplant case highlights restoring a healthy microbiota to resolve refractory C. difficile infection by reestablishing colonization resistance.

options.text: Introducing bacteriophages to directly lyse C. difficile cells
options.rationale: Wrong: FMT restores microbial diversity rather than delivering bacteriophages; phage therapy is not the lecture's described mechanism.

options.text: Delivering high-dose antibiotics via stool to sterilize gut
options.rationale: Wrong: FMT aims to repopulate beneficial microbes, not to deliver antibiotics; antibiotics can worsen dysbiosis per the lecture.

options.text: Transferring host immune cells to attack C. difficile
options.rationale: Wrong: FMT transfers microbial communities, not host immune cells; the lecture case emphasizes microbiota restoration.

options.text: Replacing host DNA to increase resistance genes
options.rationale: Wrong: FMT does not replace host DNA; it modifies microbial communities to restore colonization resistance as per the lecture.

question: Which lecture slide lists examples of Gram-positive cocci commonly found in the mouth?
cited_learning_objective: Provide examples of disease states related to microbiota at different anatomic sites.

options.text: Rothia; Staphylococcus; Streptococcus sanguinis; S. mutans
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture lists these Gram-positive cocci as part of normal oral flora, including S. mutans associated with dental caries.

options.text: Enterococcus; Peptostreptococcus; Proteus; Pseudomonas
options.rationale: Wrong: These genera are more typical of the gut or other sites; the lecture's oral Gram-positive cocci list differs.

options.text: Bacteroides; Fusobacterium; Prevotella; Actinomyces
options.rationale: Wrong: These include anaerobes and other oral organisms but the specific Gram-positive cocci listed in the lecture are Rothia and Streptococcus species.

options.text: Corynebacterium; Propionibacterium; Mycoplasma; Neisseria
options.rationale: Wrong: While some of these appear in the mouth, the lecture's Gram-positive cocci list is distinct and includes Streptococcus species.

options.text: Klebsiella; Escherichia; Salmonella; Shigella
options.rationale: Wrong: These are Gram-negative enteric rods, not the Gram-positive oral cocci named in the lecture.

question: Which lecture-described property differentiates fungi from bacteria in structural comparisons?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the classification of medically important microorganisms based on their structures.

options.text: Fungi are eukaryotic and contain ergosterol in membranes
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture notes fungi are eukaryotes with membrane sterol ergosterol, distinguishing them from bacteria.

options.text: Fungi have peptidoglycan cell walls like bacteria
options.rationale: Wrong: Fungal cell walls are complex carbohydrates, not peptidoglycan which is characteristic of bacteria.

options.text: Fungi possess 70S ribosomes similar to bacteria
options.rationale: Wrong: Fungi are eukaryotic and have 80S ribosomes; 70S ribosomes are prokaryotic.

options.text: Fungi replicate by binary fission like bacteria
options.rationale: Wrong: Fungi reproduce via mitosis/meiosis or spore formation; binary fission is a bacterial replication method.

options.text: Fungi lack a membrane-bound nucleus unlike bacteria
options.rationale: Wrong: Fungi have a membrane-bound nucleus; bacteria lack one, so this statement reverses the lecture distinctions.

question: A lab slide shows a microorganism 100 nm in size composed of nucleic acid and protein, requiring host cells to replicate. According to the lecture's size and structural categories, which group does this organism belong to?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the classification of medically important microorganisms based on their structures.

options.text: Virus as subcellular obligate intracellular agent
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture classifies viruses as 18–300 nm subcellular particles with nucleic acid and obligate intracellular replication.

options.text: Bacterium with small cell size and binary fission
options.rationale: Wrong: Bacteria are larger (typically µm scale) and replicate by binary fission; 100 nm is within viral size range per the lecture.

options.text: Protozoan unicellular eukaryote of similar size
options.rationale: Wrong: Protozoa are much larger (µm scale); 100 nm is far smaller than protozoa described in the lecture.

options.text: Fungal yeast cell of submicron dimensions
options.rationale: Wrong: Yeast are eukaryotic and typically µm-sized; 100 nm fits viral dimensions, not fungal cells.

options.text: Prion protein aggregate classified as bacterium
options.rationale: Wrong: Prions are proteinaceous infectious particles but are not classified as bacteria; the lecture groups viruses as obligate intracellular agents at this size.

question: Which lecture slide lists common Gram-negative rods found in the gastrointestinal microbiota?
cited_learning_objective: Provide examples of disease states related to microbiota at different anatomic sites.

options.text: Enterobacter; Escherichia coli; Klebsiella; Proteus
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture lists these Gram-negative rods as common gut microbiota and potential pathogens after perforation.

options.text: Staphylococcus aureus; Streptococcus viridans; Enterococcus
options.rationale: Wrong: These are Gram-positive cocci; the lecture's Gram-negative gut rods include Enterobacter and E. coli.

options.text: Lactobacillus; Bifidobacterium; Clostridium; Actinomyces
options.rationale: Wrong: These include Gram-positive and anaerobic genera; the specific Gram-negative rods named in the lecture differ.

options.text: Neisseria; Haemophilus; Bordetella; Brucella
options.rationale: Wrong: While some are Gram-negative, the lecture specifically lists Enterobacteriaceae and Pseudomonas among gut Gram-negative rods.

options.text: Bacteroides; Fusobacterium; Prevotella; Porphyromonas
options.rationale: Wrong: These are anaerobic Gram-negative genera but the lecture's listed common gut Gram-negative rods emphasized Enterobacter, E. coli, Klebsiella, and Proteus.

question: A patient with antibiotic-induced depletion of gut microbiota is challenged with an enteric pathogen. According to the lecture's figure on intestinal protection, which host factor is reduced when microbiota are depleted, thereby lowering resistance to pathogens?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Antimicrobial peptides and mucosal IgA are reduced
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture's figure shows depleted microbiota reduce antimicrobial peptides (AMPs) and IgA, compromising mucosal defenses against pathogens.

options.text: Systemic IgG levels rise to compensate for mucosal loss
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture emphasizes local mucosal defenses (AMPs, IgA) decline with microbiota depletion; systemic IgG compensation is not described.

options.text: Mucin production increases preventing pathogen contact
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture indicates mucin is part of protection, but depletion of microbiota impairs mucosal defenses rather than increasing mucin to prevent contact.

options.text: NK cell numbers in blood dramatically increase
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture figure focuses on mucosal factors like AMPs and IgA; systemic NK cell increases are not the described mechanism.

options.text: IL-22 secretion becomes excessive and protective
options.rationale: Wrong: IL-22 is part of mucosal immunity, but the lecture shows depletion reduces protective factors; excessive IL-22 is not the protective mechanism described.

question: Which lecture-listed genus is emphasized as protective in the female genital tract, and its depletion is associated with bacterial vaginosis?
cited_learning_objective: Provide examples of disease states related to microbiota at different anatomic sites.

options.text: Lactobacillus species maintain vaginal health
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture states Lactobacilli are protective in the vagina and their loss leads to dysbiosis and BV.

options.text: Klebsiella species maintain vaginal health
options.rationale: Wrong: Klebsiella are potential pathogens; the lecture identifies Lactobacillus as the protective genus in the vagina.

options.text: Escherichia coli maintain vaginal pH balance
options.rationale: Wrong: E. coli are enteric organisms; the lecture highlights Lactobacillus for vaginal pH maintenance and protection.

options.text: Bacteroides species prevent Gardnerella overgrowth
options.rationale: Wrong: Bacteroides are gut anaerobes; the lecture links Lactobacillus depletion to Gardnerella overgrowth in BV.

options.text: Propionibacterium species dominate healthy vagina
options.rationale: Wrong: Propionibacterium (Cutibacterium) is a skin commensal; the lecture names Lactobacillus as the dominant protective vaginal flora.

question: A patient undergoing broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy shows a marked decrease in Bacteroidetes and Firmicutes and a relative increase in Proteobacteria in stool sequencing. According to the lecture's antibiotic effects slide, what is the most likely immediate consequence for colonization resistance?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Reduced colonization resistance with higher pathogen risk
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture's antibiotic effects slide shows antibiotics alter community structure (loss of beneficial taxa, rise of Proteobacteria), reducing colonization resistance and increasing pathogen risk.

options.text: Enhanced colonization resistance due to Proteobacteria rise
options.rationale: Wrong: Proteobacteria expansion often reflects dysbiosis and reduced resistance; the lecture links such shifts to increased pathogen susceptibility.

options.text: Immediate restoration of mucosal IgA levels
options.rationale: Wrong: Antibiotics deplete microbiota and associated mucosal immune stimulation; immediate IgA restoration is not expected per the lecture.

options.text: Permanent elimination of C. difficile spores from gut
options.rationale: Wrong: Antibiotics can predispose to C. difficile overgrowth; they do not eliminate spores and may worsen risk as per the lecture.

options.text: Increased vitamin K synthesis protecting against infection
options.rationale: Wrong: Loss of beneficial taxa typically reduces vitamin synthesis; the lecture does not suggest antibiotics increase vitamin K production.

question: Which lecture slide concept supports the hygiene hypothesis linking early microbial exposure to immune tolerance?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Early microbial exposure builds immune tolerance and reduces allergy
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture mentions building immune tolerance (hygiene hypothesis) as a role of normal flora in shaping immune responses.

options.text: Hygiene increases IgA production causing autoimmunity
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture links microbial exposure to tolerance and immune development, not hygiene-driven IgA increases causing autoimmunity.

options.text: Lack of microbes increases mucosal antibody overproduction
options.rationale: Wrong: Germ-free models show antibody deficiencies, not overproduction; the lecture supports microbial stimulation of normal immune development.

options.text: Hygiene eliminates all commensals permanently
options.rationale: Wrong: Hygiene may reduce exposures but the lecture does not claim permanent elimination of commensals; it emphasizes developmental effects on immunity.

options.text: Early antibiotics enhance immune tolerance via dysbiosis
options.rationale: Wrong: Antibiotics cause dysbiosis and can impair immune development; the lecture does not present antibiotics as enhancing tolerance.

question: Which lecture-listed mechanism explains how normal flora can directly inhibit invading microbes by producing substances toxic to competitors?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Secretion of bacteriocins or antimicrobial compounds by flora
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture lists secretion of antibiotics or bacteriocins by normal flora as a mechanism to keep invaders out.

options.text: Host epithelial cells secreting bacteriocins independently
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture attributes bacteriocin secretion to microbes, not host epithelial cells acting independently.

options.text: Commensals phagocytosing pathogens directly
options.rationale: Wrong: Commensals inhibit via secreted antimicrobials and competition, not by phagocytosis which is a host immune function.

options.text: Production of host complement proteins by bacteria
options.rationale: Wrong: Complement is a host system; bacteria do not produce host complement proteins as described in the lecture.

options.text: Conversion of pathogens into nonviable spores by flora
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture does not describe commensals converting pathogens into spores; inhibition occurs via antimicrobials and competition.

question: A patient born by cesarean section lacks exposure to maternal vaginal flora. Based on lecture sampling findings and hypotheses, which long-term risk was suggested to be increased in such infants?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Higher risk of asthma, obesity, and autoimmune conditions
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture mentions lack of vaginal flora exposure in C-section babies may increase risk of Type 1 diabetes, celiac disease, asthma, or obesity.

options.text: Immediate development of severe neonatal sepsis
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture discusses long-term increased risks, not immediate universal neonatal sepsis from C-section delivery.

options.text: Guaranteed protection against allergic disease
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture suggests lack of exposure may increase allergy risk, not confer protection.

options.text: Permanent colonization with maternal gut flora only
options.rationale: Wrong: C-section alters initial exposure patterns; it does not guarantee permanent colonization with maternal gut flora.

options.text: Immediate overgrowth of Lactobacillus species systemically
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture links reduced vaginal flora exposure to later risks; systemic Lactobacillus overgrowth is not described.

question: Which lecture slide explicitly lists examples of anaerobic organisms commonly found in the mouth?
cited_learning_objective: Provide examples of disease states related to microbiota at different anatomic sites.

options.text: Bacteroides; Fusobacterium; Prevotella species in oral anaerobes
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture lists these anaerobic genera among oral microbiota.

options.text: Escherichia; Klebsiella; Proteus as oral anaerobes
options.rationale: Wrong: These are enteric Gram-negative rods more typical of the gut; the lecture lists different anaerobes for the mouth.

options.text: Staphylococcus aureus; Streptococcus mutans as oral anaerobes
options.rationale: Wrong: S. aureus and S. mutans are facultative or aerobic Gram-positive organisms; the lecture separates anaerobes like Bacteroides and Fusobacterium.

options.text: Lactobacillus; Bifidobacterium; Clostridium as oral aerobes
options.rationale: Wrong: These include anaerobes and gut-associated genera; the lecture's oral anaerobe list is specific to Bacteroides, Fusobacterium, Prevotella.

options.text: Neisseria; Haemophilus; Bordetella as oral anaerobes
options.rationale: Wrong: Neisseria and Haemophilus are aerobic or facultative; Bordetella is not a typical oral anaerobe per the lecture.

question: Which lecture-described outcome is most consistent with the concept of dysbiosis?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Disruption of normal microbiota leading to disease states
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture defines dysbiosis as disruption of normal microbiota that can lead to diseases like C. difficile colitis and bacterial vaginosis.

options.text: Stable increase in beneficial commensals improving health
options.rationale: Wrong: Dysbiosis denotes harmful disruption, not a stable beneficial increase in commensals.

options.text: Permanent sterilization of mucosal surfaces improving immunity
options.rationale: Wrong: Sterilization of mucosa would impair immune development; dysbiosis refers to imbalance causing disease.

options.text: Exclusive colonization by core microbiome species only
options.rationale: Wrong: Dysbiosis involves imbalance and loss of beneficial diversity, not exclusive core species colonization.

options.text: Increased microbial diversity always protecting against infection
options.rationale: Wrong: While diversity can be protective, dysbiosis is specifically harmful imbalance; increased diversity is not synonymous with dysbiosis.

question: Which lecture slide content would most directly support recommending dietary fiber (prebiotics) to improve gut metabolic health?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Prebiotics encourage growth of helpful microbes affecting metabolism
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture defines prebiotics as diet or supplements designed to encourage helpful microbes and cites literature linking dietary fiber to metabolic health.

options.text: Antibiotics are recommended to increase microbial diversity
options.rationale: Wrong: Antibiotics reduce diversity and can cause dysbiosis; the lecture supports prebiotics, not antibiotics, for metabolic benefits.

options.text: Eradication of Lactobacillus improves glucose control
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture indicates Lactobacillus is protective in the vagina and does not suggest eradication improves metabolic health.

options.text: High-fat diets are prebiotics that restore microbiota
options.rationale: Wrong: High-fat diets are not defined as prebiotics; the lecture links dietary fiber (prebiotics) to beneficial microbiota effects.

options.text: Fecal transplant is first-line for obesity prevention
options.rationale: Wrong: While FMT is discussed for C. difficile, the lecture cites dietary fiber and prebiotics for metabolic health rather than FMT as first-line obesity prevention.

question: A patient with diverticulitis has peritonitis after bowel perforation. Based on the lecture's lists of organisms, which anaerobic genus is most likely to be implicated in the resulting intra-abdominal abscess?
cited_learning_objective: Provide examples of disease states related to microbiota at different anatomic sites.

options.text: Bacteroides species commonly cause intra-abdominal abscesses
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture names Bacteroides species as anaerobes that cause intra-abdominal abscesses following intestinal perforation.

options.text: Streptococcus mutans typically causes intra-abdominal abscesses
options.rationale: Wrong: S. mutans is an oral organism linked to dental caries and endocarditis, not the classic cause of intra-abdominal abscesses after perforation.

options.text: Propionibacterium acnes is the usual intra-abdominal anaerobe
options.rationale: Wrong: Propionibacterium (Cutibacterium) is a skin commensal; the lecture implicates gut anaerobes like Bacteroides in intra-abdominal abscesses.

options.text: Neisseria species are the primary cause of peritonitis
options.rationale: Wrong: Neisseria are respiratory/oral commensals; peritonitis after bowel perforation is typically due to gut anaerobes such as Bacteroides.

options.text: Lactobacillus species usually form intra-abdominal abscesses
options.rationale: Wrong: Lactobacilli are generally protective in mucosal sites; they are not the typical pathogens causing intra-abdominal abscesses per the lecture.

question: Which lecture slide best explains why normal microbiota are considered part of primary immunity at mucosal surfaces?
cited_learning_objective: Explore the interactions between humans and normal flora in healthy and diseased states.

options.text: Normal flora occupy niches and secrete antimicrobials preventing invasion
options.rationale: Correct: The lecture states that normal flora are considered part of primary immunity by physical occupancy, secretion of bacteriocins, and maintaining environmental factors like pH.

options.text: Normal flora produce systemic IgM to neutralize pathogens
options.rationale: Wrong: The lecture emphasizes local mucosal mechanisms (occupancy, bacteriocins, pH), not production of systemic IgM by microbes.

options.text: Normal flora directly phagocytose invading bacteria at mucosa
options.rationale: Wrong: Phagocytosis is a host immune cell function; the lecture attributes barrier and competitive functions to flora.

options.text: Normal flora form permanent biofilms in sterile tissues
options.rationale: Wrong: Biofilms on mucosal surfaces can be protective, but permanent colonization of sterile tissues would be pathogenic, not primary immunity.

options.text: Normal flora secrete host complement proteins at mucosa
options.rationale: Wrong: Complement is produced by the host; microbes do not secrete host complement proteins as described in the lecture.
